payload.text:
I want to build a google sheet based BLO Account Management System 
Google Sheet Link - https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/199EWhTKl3E1MxSSL2-xFFIHrvhKJnADLCtLyWI4pSMc/edit?usp=sharing
There are 4 sheets
1. User - This sheet will have user login related data there are two type of user - admin and tehsil. Admin user will have full access to the portal while tehsil user can see only data related to their tehsil. 
Columns 
User_ID - this column will be unique and user to map user to BLO_Account sheet.
User_Name - this will be used for login purpose
Password - this will be used for login purpose
User_Type - admin or tehsil. admin user will have full access while tehsil user can access based on mapping
Officer_Name, Designation, Mobile - these are user details columns will be used to show on dashboard user name and details after login 
T_STMP_ADD - updated when record added first time
T_STMP_UPD - updated when record gets updated

2. BLO_Account - this is main data table where all account related information will be saved which will be entered from tehsil users.
Columns
BLO_ID - unique identifier for record
User_ID - this is the user mapping for filteration purpose
AC_No - assembly number
AC_Name - assembly name
Tehsil - tehsil name
Part_No - part number
Part_Name_EN - part name in english
Part_Name_HI - part name in hindi
BLO_Name - BLO name
Gender - Gender of BLO
Designation - designation detail
Department - department detail
Mobile - mobile number
EPIC - voter id number
Bank_ID - this will be mapped to Bank sheet using Bank_ID
Branch_ID - this will be mapped to Bank_Branch sheet using Branch_ID
IFSC_Code - IFSC Code 
Account_Number - Account number of BLO
Account_Passbook_Doc - user will upload passbook photo or pdf here. it should be uploaded to google drive and a viewable link will be updated to google sheet into this column
Verified - after entry there will be a seperate module to verify each entry done. after successful verification it will be set yes
T_STMP_ADD - updated when a record gets added first time
T_STMP_UPD - updated when a record gets updated

3. Bank - 

Columns
Bank_ID - Bank record unique identifier 
Bank_Name - Bank Name
T_STMP_ADD - updated when a record gets added first time
T_STMP_UPD - updated when a record gets updated

4. Bank_Branch

Columns
Branch_ID - Bank branch record unique identifier
Branch_Name - bank branch name 
IFSC_Code - IFSC Code
Bank_ID - mapped to Bank sheet using Bank_ID
T_STMP_ADD - updated when a record gets added first time
T_STMP_UPD - updated when a record gets updated

First it will have user login page 
after successful login there will be below modules
1. Bank Account Entry Module - It will show data in list form and give an edit button to update entry. Columns which will be shown in sections 
Assembly Details Section - AC_No, AC_Name, Tehsil, Part_No, Part_Name_EN, Part_Name_HI
BLO Details Section - BLO_Name, Gender, Designation, Department, Mobile, EPIC
Account Details Section - IFSC_Code(web based search facility), Account_Number, Account_Passbook_Doc (upload option), Bank_ID, Branch_ID will be populated based on IFSC_Code search
IFSC code search facility using web, if any bank or branch is not in google sheet it will add it to google sheet then do entry or update. 
2. Verification Module will show summarized BLO details and PDF/JPG viewing screen so that user can match entered account number with passbook document uploaded by user. If user verifies it then editing will be froze for that record. Only admin can un-verify these records.

Use Modern UI using bootstrap css and other modern css templates. It should be mobile device friendly. Have a sidebar layout which can be made hidden in mobile devices.

**Defining Project Scope**

Okay, I'm aiming for a "BLO Account Management System," built with React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. The core challenge now is defining the precise scope. The project mimics a Google Sheets backend with mock data/services. I'm focusing on defining the key functionalities.


**Refining Core Functionality**

I've been drilling down on the essential features for the "BLO Account Management System." Currently, the focus is on structuring data access to be Gemini-ready, which, in practice, means making a "Google Sheet without a backend proxy." I've finalized the core data models including `User`, `BLO_Account`, `Bank`, and `Bank_Branch`. I'm starting to build out a list of features, including a role-based login, a mobile-responsive dashboard, and an account entry module.


**Outlining Key Modules**

Now, I'm fleshing out specific modules. I have `geminiService.ts` for IFSC validation (leveraging Gemini's parsing capabilities, which will replace a traditional API call). Navigation is in `Sidebar.tsx`, the app wrapper in `Layout.tsx`, and the login UI in `Login.tsx`. I have `BLOList.tsx` for account lists, `AccountEntry.tsx` for the edit form, and `Verification.tsx` for the verification module. I'm using Tailwind CSS for all styling and React Icons (specifically Lucide-react). I'm implementing a responsive drawer for mobile. I am adding functional updates for state to avoid loops.

I've also implemented some features for Auth with mock logic checking user data from a sheet. Finally, Tehsil users see filtered account data, while admins see all. There's also a function for IFSC search.

**Investigating IFSC Code Inconsistencies**

I'm currently focused on why the `searchIFSCViaGemini` function returns inconsistent results for the same IFSC code. It's using the Gemini API, specifically `gemini-3-flash-preview`. The core issue is the fluctuating responses from the LLM, despite identical inputs. This lack of stability poses a significant challenge, requiring further investigation to pinpoint the root cause, whether in the prompt, the model itself, or the API's implementation.


**Addressing LLM Variability**

I'm now thinking about the probabilistic nature of LLMs, and why I'm seeing inconsistent results. Even with identical prompts, these models can vary significantly. I'm focusing on grounding the responses with strict canonical naming and setting the temperature to zero in Gemini to reduce these variations. The goal is to ensure consistency, especially since I'm already using local searches first. The user needs reliable and repeatable bank and branch names.

The reason you are seeing different results for the same IFSC code is due to the **stochastic (probabilistic) nature** of Large Language Models. By default, the model has a "temperature" (randomness) setting that allows it to be creative. For technical data like IFSC codes, we need the model to be **deterministic**—meaning it should always give the exact same answer for the exact same input.

### Specification of Updates:

1.  **Zero Randomness (Deterministic Mode)**: I will update the `geminiService.ts` to set `temperature: 0`. This tells the model to always pick the most likely "correct" token rather than varying its response.
2.  **System Instruction**: I will add a strict system instruction to the API call. This instructs the model to act as a verified banking database and to use canonical (official) names only, avoiding abbreviations like "Ltd" or "Pvt" unless they are part of the standard name.
3.  **Strict Schema Enforcement**: I will tighten the prompt to ensure it doesn't try to "chat" or explain its reasoning, which can sometimes pollute the JSON output.

entries.description: Set temperature to 0 and added a system instruction to ensure deterministic and consistent IFSC search results.

payload.text:
### What this change does:
*   **`temperature: 0`**: Eliminates the "creativity" of the AI. Now, if you ask for the same IFSC code 100 times, it will calculate the most statistically certain answer 100 times, which will be identical.
*   **`topP: 0.1`**: Further restricts the model from wandering off to less-likely words.
*   **Uppercase Normalization**: I added a `.toUpperCase()` call in the service return. This ensures that even if the model returns "Hdfc Bank" or "HDFC BANK", the application always receives "HDFC BANK", preventing duplicate entries caused by casing differences.

Building a dedicated mobile app for BLOs would involve a few key architectural choices:

1.  **Framework**: We could use **React Native** or **Flutter** for a native feel, or a **Progressive Web App (PWA)** if you want users to install it directly from a browser without using an App Store.
2.  **OTP Authentication**: We would integrate a service like **Firebase Auth**, **Twilio**, or a regional SMS gateway (like **Msg91**). The BLO would enter their mobile number, receive a 6-digit code, and be instantly logged into their specific assigned parts.
3.  **Simplified UI**: The interface would be "mobile-first," featuring large buttons, a simplified "one-field-at-a-time" entry form, and direct camera integration so BLOs can snap photos of passbooks on the spot.
4.  **Backend Integration**: The app would connect to your existing **Google Sheets/Apps Script backend**, ensuring all data remains synchronized between the mobile users and the central administrators.
5.  **Offline Support**: Since BLOs often work in areas with poor connectivity, we can implement **local caching**. They could save data offline, and the app would automatically sync to the cloud once they return to a 4G/5G zone.

**Assessing OTP Costs**

I'm now zeroing in on the financial implications of OTP authentication, specifically SMS-based OTPs. My focus is narrowing to understand the direct costs the user might incur, given they want to implement OTP in their app. I'm considering the per-message fees associated with sending these verification codes.


**Analyzing Authentication Expenses**

I've been analyzing the expense structure of different OTP authentication methods. I'm noting that SMS gateways always incur costs, typically a per-message fee charged by providers like Twilio or regional players in India. I'm comparing this to Google's Firebase Authentication, which offers a free tier (though its availability fluctuates by region and pricing model). I'm also considering alternatives like email OTPs (free but requires an email) and WhatsApp API (usually paid), and simple password authentication (free). I want to break this cost down into platform and SMS delivery components.

Yes, **standard SMS-based OTP authentication usually costs money**, though there are ways to keep it free or very low-cost depending on your scale.

Here is the breakdown of the costs you would encounter:

### 1. The "Free" Option (Firebase Authentication)
Google's **Firebase** is the most common choice for developers.
*   **The Good News:** They offer a "Spark Plan" (Free Tier) that allows for a certain number of phone verifications per month at no cost.
*   **The Catch:** This limit is usually around **10,000 verifications per month** globally, but in some regions (like India), Google recently moved towards a "pay-as-you-go" model for Identity Platform where after a very small free allowance, you are charged per successful verification.

### 2. Direct SMS Gateway Costs (e.g., Msg91, Twilio, TextLocal)
If you don't use Firebase and use a direct SMS provider:
*   **Per Message Cost:** You pay for every single SMS sent. In India, this is typically between **₹0.12 to ₹0.25 per SMS**.
*   **DLT Registration (India Specific):** To send SMS in India, you must register your "Entity" and "Header" (the sender name like `DEO-BLD`) on a DLT platform (like Vilpower or JIO). This involves a one-time registration fee (usually around **₹5,000 + GST**) and strict template approvals.

### 3. Hidden "Administrative" Costs
*   **International Rates:** If you use a US-based service like Twilio to send messages to Indian numbers, it is significantly more expensive (often $0.02 - $0.05 per message) than using a local Indian provider.

### How to do it for 100% FREE?
If you want to avoid costs entirely for your BLO app, I recommend these alternatives:

1.  **Mobile Number + Fixed PIN:** Instead of an OTP, give each BLO a pre-assigned 4-digit or 6-digit PIN (stored in your Google Sheet). It’s just as easy for them, but costs ₹0 to "send."
2.  **WhatsApp "OTP":** While the official WhatsApp API costs money, some developers use simpler integrations, but even these eventually have a price tag.
3.  **Email OTP:** If your BLOs have email addresses, sending an OTP via email is **completely free** using Google Apps Script (which you are already using).

**My Recommendation:** For a government-related project (BLO system), the **DLT registration** and **SMS costs** are usually the biggest headache. Using a **Mobile Number + Secret PIN** system (verified against your existing Sheet) is the most cost-effective and fastest way to build it without a budget.
